preamble.summary: A communal lament by Asaph over the destruction of the temple. The enemy has smashed the sanctuary's carved woodwork, set fire to the holy place, and desecrated every meeting place of God in the land. No prophet remains to say how long the desolation will last. The psalmist appeals to God's primordial power as Creator who crushed the sea monsters and established the cosmic order, then pleads: remember Your covenant, rise up, and defend Your cause.
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 74 contains one of the Hebrew Bible's most vivid mythological passages (verses 12-17), where God is portrayed as a warrior who crushes Leviathan's heads, splits open springs and rivers, and establishes the boundaries of day and night. This is creation described not as peaceful ordering but as combat — God defeating the chaos monsters to establish the world. The psalmist deploys this ancient combat myth strategically: if You did that, surely You can handle these human enemies. The juxtaposition of cosmic power with national helplessness is the psalm's rhetorical engine.
preamble.friction: The psalm assumes a destroyed temple, which most scholars associate with the Babylonian destruction of 586 BCE. However, the Asaph attribution and some linguistic features have led others to propose the Assyrian period or even the Maccabean era. The mythological language of verses 12-17 draws on Canaanite creation mythology (the defeat of Yam/Sea and the multi-headed Lotan/Leviathan known from Ugaritic texts), which the psalmist appropriates for YHWH without apology. This is not syncretism but theological conquest — Israel's God is the one who actually did what the Canaanite myths attributed to Baal.
preamble.connections: The Leviathan imagery connects to Job 41, Isaiah 27:1, and the Ugaritic Baal cycle where Baal defeats Lotan (the seven-headed sea serpent). The lament over the destroyed temple parallels Lamentations 1-2 and Psalm 79. The appeal to the covenant (verse 20) connects to the Deuteronomic tradition of covenant faithfulness despite national disaster. The phrase 'How long?' (ad matai) echoes Psalm 13, Psalm 79:5, and Habakkuk 1:2.

verses.text_hebrew: מַשְׂכִּ֗יל לְאָ֫סָ֥ף לָמָ֣ה אֱ֭לֹהִים זָנַ֣חְתָּ לָנֶ֑צַח יֶעְשַׁ֥ן אַ֝פְּךָ֗ בְּצֹ֣אן מַרְעִיתֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: O God, why hast thou cast us off for ever? why doth thine anger smoke against the sheep of thy pasture?
verses.rendering:
A maskil of Asaph.

Why, O God, have You rejected us forever?
Why does Your anger smoke
against the sheep of Your pasture?
translator_notes: The term maskil in the superscription likely indicates a skillful or instructive composition. Its exact meaning is debated, but it appears in thirteen psalm superscriptions and may signal a psalm requiring particular artistry in performance.

verses.text_hebrew: זְכֹ֤ר עֲדָתְךָ֨ ׀ קָ֘נִ֤יתָ קֶּ֗דֶם גָּ֭אַלְתָּ שֵׁ֣בֶט נַחֲלָתֶ֑ךָ הַר־צִ֝יּ֗וֹן זֶ֣ה שָׁכַ֣נְתָּ בּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Remember thy congregation, which thou hast purchased of old; the rod of thine inheritance, which thou hast redeemed; this mount Zion, wherein thou hast dwelt.
verses.rendering:
Remember Your congregation that You acquired long ago,
the tribe of Your inheritance that You redeemed —
Mount Zion where You have dwelt.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb zekhor ('remember') is the psalm's central imperative — it appears again in verses 18 and 22. To 'remember' in Hebrew is not merely to recall but to act on what is recalled. The psalmist reminds God of three things: qanita qedem ('You acquired long ago') — God purchased this people; ga'alta ('You redeemed') — God acted as kinsman-redeemer; and har Tsiyyon zeh shakhanta bo ('Mount Zion where You have dwelt') — God chose to live here. Every argument is relational: these are Your people, this is Your mountain.
translator_notes: The word shevet ('tribe, rod, staff') here means 'tribe' — the tribe of God's inheritance, meaning Israel as God's own clan or family. The language of qanah ('to acquire, to purchase, to create') echoes Exodus 15:16 and Deuteronomy 32:6.

key_terms.semantic_range: to redeem, to act as kinsman-redeemer, to buy back, to reclaim
key_terms.note: The go'el is the family member responsible for reclaiming what belongs to the family. When applied to God, it means God treats Israel as His own kin — their losses are His losses, their enslavement is a family matter He must resolve.

verses.text_hebrew: הָרִ֣ימָה פְ֭עָמֶיךָ לְמַשֻּׁא֣וֹת נֶ֑צַח כׇּל־הֵ֝רַ֗ע אוֹיֵ֥ב בַּקֹּֽדֶשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: Lift up thy feet unto the perpetual desolations; even all that the enemy hath done wickedly in the sanctuary.
verses.rendering:
Lift Your steps toward the perpetual ruins —
all the destruction the enemy has wrought in the sanctuary.
translator_notes: The phrase harimah pe'amekha ('lift Your steps') is a plea for God to physically walk to the ruins and see what has happened. The word mashu'ot ('ruins, desolations') with netsach ('perpetual') suggests the destruction has been standing long enough to feel permanent. God is being invited to survey the damage.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׁאֲג֣וּ צֹ֭רְרֶיךָ בְּקֶ֣רֶב מוֹעֲדֶ֑ךָ שָׂ֖מוּ אוֹתֹתָ֣ם אֹתֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: Thine enemies roar in the midst of thy congregations; they set up their ensigns for signs.
verses.rendering:
Your enemies have roared in the midst of Your meeting place;
they set up their own signs as signs.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word sha'agu ('they roared') describes the battle cry of invaders in the very place where God was supposed to meet His people. The mo'ed ('meeting place, appointed assembly') is sacred space — the place of divine-human encounter. The enemies' ototam otot ('their signs as signs') means they replaced God's symbols with their own military or religious emblems. The holy place has been profaned not just physically but symbolically — foreign signs now stand where God's presence was marked.
translator_notes: The wordplay on otot ('signs') is pointed: God's signs (the covenant markers, the sacred furnishings) have been replaced by the enemy's signs (military standards, pagan emblems). The desecration is total — even the semiotics of the space have been overwritten.

verses.text_hebrew: יִ֭וָּדַע כְּמֵבִ֣יא לְמָ֑עְלָה בִּ֝סְבׇךְ־עֵ֗ץ קַרְדֻּמּֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: A man was famous according as he had lifted up axes upon the thick trees.
verses.rendering:
It was like men wielding axes
in a thicket of trees.
translator_notes: The verse is syntactically difficult, but the image is clear: the invaders attacked the temple's ornate woodwork as if they were lumberjacks felling a forest. The carved panels and cedar work that adorned the sanctuary were hacked apart with axes (qardummot). The comparison to forest clearing emphasizes the brutality — sacred art treated as timber.

verses.text_hebrew: וְ֭עַתָּ֗ה פִּתּוּחֶ֥יהָ יָּ֑חַד בְּכַשִּׁ֥יל וְ֝כֵילַפֹּ֗ת יַהֲלֹמֽוּן׃
verses.text_kjv: But now they break down the carved work thereof at once with axes and hammers.
verses.rendering:
And now — its carved work, all of it,
they smash with hatchet and hammer.
translator_notes: The pittuchim ('carved engravings') likely refer to the elaborate carved panels of cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers described in 1 Kings 6:29. The kashil ('hatchet') and keilapot ('hammers, pickaxes') are the blunt instruments of demolition. The beauty that Solomon's artisans spent years creating is being smashed in moments.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁלְח֣וּ בָ֭אֵשׁ מִקְדָּשֶׁ֑ךָ לָ֝אָ֗רֶץ חִלְּל֥וּ מִשְׁכַּן־שְׁמֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: They have cast fire into thy sanctuary, they have defiled by casting down the dwelling place of thy name to the ground.
verses.rendering:
They set Your sanctuary on fire;
to the ground they profaned the dwelling place of Your name.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb shilchu va-esh ('they sent fire into') describes arson — deliberate burning of sacred space. The phrase mishkan shemekha ('the dwelling place of Your name') is the Deuteronomic theology of the temple: God's name resides there (Deuteronomy 12:5). To burn the temple is to attack God's name, His reputation, His presence in the world. The verb chillelu ('they profaned, desecrated') means to treat holy things as common — the ultimate violation.
translator_notes: The phrase la-arets ('to the ground') indicates total destruction — not mere damage but complete demolition. The Babylonian destruction of 586 BCE matches this description precisely (2 Kings 25:9).

verses.text_hebrew: אָמְר֣וּ בְ֭לִבָּם נִינָ֣ם יָ֑חַד שָׂרְפ֖וּ כׇל־מוֹעֲדֵי־אֵ֣ל בָּאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: They said in their hearts, Let us destroy them together: they have burned up all the synagogues of God in the land.
verses.rendering:
They said in their hearts, 'Let us crush them altogether.'
They burned every meeting place of God in the land.
translator_notes: The phrase kol mo'adei El ba-arets ('every meeting place of God in the land') is significant — it suggests not only the Jerusalem temple but all locations where God was worshiped. If this refers to local sanctuaries, synagogues, or regional gathering places, it indicates systematic, nationwide religious destruction.

verses.text_hebrew: אוֹתֹתֵ֗ינוּ לֹ֣א רָ֭אִינוּ אֵין־ע֣וֹד נָבִ֑יא וְלֹא־אִ֝תָּ֗נוּ יֹדֵ֥עַ עַד־מָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: We see not our signs: there is no more any prophet: neither is there among us any that knoweth how long.
verses.expanded_rendering: Three devastating absences: ototenu lo ra'inu ('our signs we do not see') — God's markers of presence have vanished; ein od navi ('there is no more a prophet') — the channel of divine communication has been severed; velo ittanu yodea ad mah ('and no one among us knows until when') — the community has lost not just God's presence and God's word but even the ability to know when the suffering will end. This is the darkest possible spiritual condition: God is silent, His signs are gone, and no one can say if relief will come.
translator_notes: The absence of prophecy is the most terrifying element. In a culture where prophets mediated God's word, the disappearance of prophetic voices meant the complete breakdown of communication between heaven and earth. Amos 8:11-12 describes this as a 'famine of hearing the words of the LORD.'

verses.text_hebrew: עַד־מָתַ֣י אֱ֭לֹהִים יְחָ֣רֶף צָ֑ר יְנָאֵ֥ץ א֝וֹיֵ֗ב שִׁמְךָ֥ לָנֶֽצַח׃
verses.text_kjv: O God, how long shall the adversary reproach? shall the enemy blaspheme thy name for ever?
verses.rendering:
How long, O God, will the foe mock?
Will the enemy scorn Your name forever?
translator_notes: The phrase ad matai ('how long?') is the quintessential lament question — it appears throughout the Psalms and prophets. The verbs yecharef ('will mock, reproach') and yena'ets ('will scorn, blaspheme') target God's shem ('name') — the enemy's mockery is not merely of Israel but of Israel's God.

verses.text_hebrew: לָ֤מָּה תָשִׁ֣יב יָ֭דְךָ וִֽימִינֶ֑ךָ מִקֶּ֖רֶב חֵיקְךָ֣ כַלֵּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Why withdrawest thou thy hand, even thy right hand? pluck it out of thy bosom.
verses.rendering:
Why do You hold back Your hand?
Your right hand — pull it from Your chest and destroy!
verses.expanded_rendering: The image is vivid and almost confrontational: God has tucked His right hand (the hand of action, of power) into His bosom, as if warming it or hiding it. The psalmist demands that He pull it out and act. The imperative kalleh ('finish them, destroy, consume') is a command directed at God — the psalmist is not asking politely but demanding decisive intervention. This is the boldness of lament — addressing God with an urgency that borders on disrespect but is born of desperate faith.
translator_notes: The image of God hiding His hand in His robe while His people suffer is one of the Psalter's most daring anthropomorphisms. It implies that God has the power to act but is choosing not to — a charge the psalmist levels directly.

verses.text_hebrew: וֵאלֹהִ֣ים מַ֭לְכִּי מִקֶּ֑דֶם פֹּעֵ֥ל יְ֝שׁוּע֗וֹת בְּקֶ֣רֶב הָאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: For God is my King of old, working salvation in the midst of the earth.
verses.rendering:
Yet God is my King from of old,
working salvation in the midst of the earth.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm pivots from lament to myth — from present devastation to primordial triumph. The word mi-qedem ('from of old, from the ancient past') reaches back before history to the foundations of the world. The phrase po'el yeshu'ot ('working salvations') uses the plural — not one act of salvation but a pattern of saving interventions. The phrase beqerev ha-arets ('in the midst of the earth') locates God's cosmic work in the visible, physical world. What follows (verses 13-17) is the evidence.
translator_notes: This verse introduces the mythological hymn that forms the psalm's theological core (verses 12-17). The psalmist counters present despair with memory of God's ultimate demonstrations of power.

verses.text_hebrew: אַתָּ֤ה פוֹרַ֣רְתָּ בְעׇזְּךָ֣ יָ֑ם שִׁבַּ֖רְתָּ רָאשֵׁ֥י תַ֝נִּינִ֗ים עַל־הַמָּֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou didst divide the sea by thy strength: thou brakest the heads of the dragons in the waters.
verses.rendering:
You split the sea by Your strength;
You shattered the heads of the sea monsters on the waters.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb porarta ('You split, You broke apart') describes violent division — God did not gently part the sea but tore it open. The tanninim ('sea monsters, dragons, great serpents') are the chaos creatures that inhabit the primordial deep. Their 'heads' (plural) indicate multiple creatures or a multi-headed monster. This is creation as combat: God establishing order by defeating the powers of chaos. The imagery draws on the same mythological tradition as the Ugaritic texts where Baal defeats Yam (Sea) — but here it is YHWH who does it.
translator_notes: The tanninim appear in Genesis 1:21 as creatures God creates, but here they appear as enemies God defeats. The two traditions coexist in the Hebrew Bible: creation as peaceful ordering (Genesis 1) and creation as combat victory (Psalm 74, Isaiah 51:9-10, Job 26:12-13).

key_terms.semantic_range: sea monsters, dragons, great serpents, chaos creatures of the deep
key_terms.note: The tanninim represent primordial chaos — the forces of disorder that threatened to prevent creation. God's defeat of these creatures is the foundational act that makes the ordered world possible. In the psalmist's rhetoric, if God can defeat cosmic chaos, surely He can defeat Babylon.

verses.text_hebrew: אַתָּ֣ה רִ֭צַּצְתָּ רָאשֵׁ֣י לִוְיָתָ֑ן תִּתְּנֶ֥נּוּ מַ֝אֲכָ֗ל לְעָ֣ם לְצִיִּֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou brakest the heads of leviathan in pieces, and gavest him to be meat to the people inhabiting the wilderness.
verses.rendering:
You crushed the heads of Leviathan;
You gave him as food to the creatures of the desert.
verses.expanded_rendering: Leviathan (livyatan) is the great sea serpent, cognate with the Ugaritic Lotan — the seven-headed dragon that Baal defeated according to Canaanite mythology. The psalmist claims this victory for YHWH: attah ritsatsta ('You — You crushed') with emphatic pronoun. The crushing of multiple 'heads' (rashei, plural) matches the Ugaritic description of Lotan as multi-headed. The defeated monster is fed to le-am le-tsiyyim ('to a people, to desert creatures') — even the carcass of chaos becomes sustenance. God wastes nothing.
translator_notes: The Ugaritic parallel is striking: KTU 1.5.I.1-3 describes Baal defeating Lotan, the fleeing serpent, the twisting serpent, the tyrant with seven heads. Psalm 74:14 transfers this exact mythology to YHWH. Isaiah 27:1 uses nearly identical language for a future eschatological defeat of Leviathan.

verses.text_hebrew: אַתָּ֣ה בָ֭קַעְתָּ מַעְיָ֣ן וָנָ֑חַל אַתָּ֥ה ה֝וֹבַ֗שְׁתָּ נַהֲר֥וֹת אֵיתָֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou didst cleave the fountain and the flood: thou driedst up mighty rivers.
verses.rendering:
You split open spring and torrent;
You dried up ever-flowing rivers.
translator_notes: The verb baqa'ta ('You split open') describes God creating water sources — springs (ma'yan) and torrents (nachal) — by cracking open the earth. But God also controls water by removing it: hovashta naharot eitan ('You dried up mighty rivers'). God both provides and withholds water. This double mastery over water connects creation to Exodus — splitting the sea and drying the Jordan.

verses.text_hebrew: לְךָ֣ י֭וֹם אַף־לְךָ֥ לָ֑יְלָה אַתָּ֥ה הֲ֝כִינ֗וֹתָ מָא֥וֹר וָשָֽׁמֶשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: The day is thine, the night also is thine: thou hast prepared the light and the sun.
verses.rendering:
Yours is the day, Yours also the night;
You established the light and the sun.
translator_notes: The sovereignty extends to time itself. The word ma'or ('luminary, light-bearer') may refer to the moon (as in Genesis 1:16, 'the lesser light'), paired with shemesh ('sun'). God owns every hour and created every source of light. The declaration lekha yom af lekha laylah ('Yours is the day, also Yours the night') means there is no time — not even darkness — outside God's dominion.

verses.text_hebrew: אַתָּ֣ה הִ֭צַּבְתָּ כׇּל־גְּב֣וּלוֹת אָ֑רֶץ קַ֥יִץ וָ֝חֹ֗רֶף אַתָּ֥ה יְצַרְתָּֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou hast set all the borders of the earth: thou hast made summer and winter.
verses.rendering:
You fixed all the boundaries of the earth;
summer and winter — You formed them.
verses.expanded_rendering: The mythological hymn concludes with God's mastery over geography and seasons. The word gevulot ('boundaries, borders') includes the boundaries between land and sea, between nations, between biomes — every limit and demarcation in the created order. Qayits va-choref ('summer and winter') represents the entire seasonal cycle. The verb yetsartam ('You formed them') uses the same verb as the creation of humanity in Genesis 2:7 (vayyitser). God is the potter who shapes time and space alike.
translator_notes: The rhetorical strategy of verses 12-17 is now complete: the psalmist has established God's absolute sovereignty over chaos monsters, water, time, light, geography, and seasons. The implicit question is: if You command all of this, why do You tolerate the destruction of Your temple?

verses.text_hebrew: זְכׇר־זֹ֗את א֭וֹיֵב חֵרֵ֣ף ׀ יְהוָ֑ה וְעַ֥ם נָ֝בָ֗ל נִֽאֲצ֥וּ שְׁמֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Remember this, that the enemy hath reproached, O LORD, and that the foolish people have blasphemed thy name.
verses.rendering:
Remember this: the enemy has mocked, O LORD,
and a foolish people has scorned Your name.
translator_notes: The imperative zekhor ('remember') returns from verse 2. The psalmist uses God's own honor as leverage: it is not merely Israel that has been insulted but YHWH's name. The word naval ('foolish, senseless, morally vile') is the same word used to describe Nabal in 1 Samuel 25:25 — a person whose folly is moral, not intellectual.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תִּתֵּ֣ן לְ֭חַיַּת נֶ֣פֶשׁ תּוֹרֶ֑ךָ חַיַּ֥ת עֲ֝נִיֶּ֗יךָ אַל־תִּשְׁכַּ֥ח לָנֶֽצַח׃
verses.text_kjv: O deliver not the soul of thy turtledove unto the multitude of the wicked: forget not the congregation of thy poor for ever.
verses.rendering:
Do not give the life of Your dove to the wild beast;
do not forget the life of Your afflicted ones forever.
verses.expanded_rendering: The image is tender and desperate: Israel is a turtledove (tor) — small, vulnerable, gentle — and the enemies are chayyat ('wild beasts'). The plea is for God not to hand His dove over to predators. The word aniyyekha ('Your afflicted ones') returns from the psalm's opening section — these are God's own poor, His own suffering people. The final word lanetsach ('forever') echoes verse 1: the psalmist fears that the abandonment is permanent.
translator_notes: The turtledove (tor) was also a sacrificial bird used by the poor who could not afford a lamb (Leviticus 5:7). The identification of Israel as a turtledove suggests both vulnerability and sacrificial status — the people have become an offering, but not a willing one.

verses.text_hebrew: הַבֵּ֥ט לַבְּרִ֑ית כִּ֤י מָלְא֨וּ מַחֲשַׁכֵּי־אֶ֗רֶץ נְא֣וֹת חָמָֽס׃
verses.text_kjv: Have respect unto the covenant: for the dark places of the earth are full of the habitations of cruelty.
verses.rendering:
Look to the covenant,
for the dark places of the land are filled
with dens of violence.
verses.expanded_rendering: The single imperative habbet labberit ('look to the covenant') is the psalm's most concentrated theological argument. Everything rests on this: God made a covenant, and covenants carry obligations. The berit is God's binding commitment — He cannot abandon it without violating His own character. The supporting clause — machashakhei erets ('the dark places of the earth/land') are filled with ne'ot chamas ('pastures/habitations of violence') — paints a picture of a land where darkness and violence have occupied every space that should belong to peace.
translator_notes: The word ne'ot can mean 'pastures' or 'dwelling places' — both readings work. If 'pastures,' the image is of grazing lands turned into sites of violence; if 'dwelling places,' the dark corners of the land have become dens where cruelty lives.

key_terms.semantic_range: covenant, treaty, agreement, binding commitment, pact
key_terms.note: The berit is the foundational relationship between God and Israel — a solemn, binding agreement with obligations on both sides. The psalmist's appeal to the covenant is an appeal to God's own sworn word: You promised. The specific covenant referenced may be the Abrahamic (Genesis 15), Mosaic (Exodus 24), or Davidic (2 Samuel 7).

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־יָשֹׁ֣ב דַּ֣ךְ נִכְלָ֑ם עָנִ֥י וְ֝אֶבְי֗וֹן יְהַלְל֥וּ שְׁמֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: O let not the oppressed return ashamed: let the poor and needy praise thy name.
verses.rendering:
Do not let the crushed turn back in shame;
let the afflicted and needy praise Your name.
translator_notes: The word dakh ('crushed, oppressed, broken') describes someone who has been physically beaten down. The plea is that this person not also be humiliated (nikhlam, 'put to shame'). The alternative — ani ve-evyon yehalelu shemekha ('the afflicted and needy will praise Your name') — promises that rescue will produce worship. God's intervention serves God's own name.

verses.text_hebrew: ק֣וּמָה אֱ֭לֹהִים רִיבָ֣ה רִיבֶ֑ךָ זְכֹ֥ר חֶרְפָּתְךָ֥ מִנִּי נָבָ֗ל כׇּל־הַיּֽוֹם׃
verses.text_kjv: Arise, O God, plead thine own cause: remember how the foolish man reproacheth thee daily.
verses.rendering:
Rise up, O God, defend Your own cause!
Remember the mockery against You
from the foolish all day long.
verses.expanded_rendering: The final appeal is not 'defend us' but rivah rivekha ('defend Your own cause, plead Your own case'). The psalmist makes God's honor the issue at stake. The mockery is not merely against Israel but against God — cherpatkha ('Your reproach, the insult against You'). The word naval ('foolish, senseless') appears again from verse 18, forming an inclusio. The psalm ends not with resolution but with urgent, unanswered demand.
translator_notes: The psalm does not record God's answer. Like many laments, it ends in the act of pleading. The resolution is left to God and to history — the psalmist has made his case and now waits.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תִּ֭שְׁכַּח ק֣וֹל צֹרְרֶ֑יךָ שְׁא֥וֹן קָ֝מֶ֗יךָ עֹלֶ֥ה תָמִֽיד׃
verses.text_kjv: Forget not the voice of thine enemies: the tumult of those that rise up against thee increaseth continually.
verses.rendering:
Do not forget the clamor of Your foes —
the uproar of those who rise against You,
which ascends continually.
translator_notes: The psalm's final word is tamid ('continually') — the enemies' noise never stops. This is a deliberate contrast with the silence of verse 9 (no prophet, no signs, no knowledge). God's people are silent; God's enemies are loud. The psalm ends with this unbearable imbalance and trusts God to correct it.
